content.full_text:
1-17-77
p.2

Section 8/207
M-97-508

Trustee with Legal Title to Trust Assets

Many trustees have legal title to all of the assets of

trusts which they administer. A trustee who has legal title, but

no beneficial interest in the trust is not deemed to own any por-

tion of the trust "directly, indirectly or beneficially". Ac-

cordingly, a public official, who as a trustee possesses legal

title to trust assets, need not disclose such assets or disqualify

himself from governmental decisions which may materially affect

such assets.

However, if the trust were a source of income to the

trustee by virtue of payments made to him for administering the

trust, he would be required to disclose the trust as a source of

income and disqualify himself from any governmental decision which

may materially affect the trust. He still would not have to dis-

close the assets of the trust nor disqualify himself from govern-

mental decisions which could materially affect an individual

trust asset but which would not materially affect the trust as

a whole.

sections.conclusion: A trustee who has legal title but no beneficial interest in a trust is not deemed to own any portion of the trust "directly, indirectly or beneficially" and therefore need not disclose such assets or disqualify from governmental decisions affecting them, unless the trust is a source of income to the trustee.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a public official serving as trustee with legal title to trust assets must disclose such assets and disqualify from governmental decisions affecting them.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A public official who is a trustee with legal title but no beneficial interest need not disclose trust assets or disqualify from decisions affecting them, unless the trust provides income to the trustee, in which case disclosure and disqualification are required only at the trust level, not individual assets.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a public official serving as trustee with legal title to trust assets must disclose such assets and disqualify from governmental decisions affecting them.

CONCLUSION: A trustee who has legal title but no beneficial interest in a trust is not deemed to own any portion of the trust "directly, indirectly or beneficially" and therefore need not disclose such assets or disqualify from governmental decisions affecting them, unless the trust is a source of income to the trustee.
embedding.first_500_words: 1-17-77 p.2 Section 8/207 M-97-508 Trustee with Legal Title to Trust Assets Many trustees have legal title to all of the assets of trusts which they administer. A trustee who has legal title, but no beneficial interest in the trust is not deemed to own any por- tion of the trust "directly, indirectly or beneficially". Ac- cordingly, a public official, who as a trustee possesses legal title to trust assets, need not disclose such assets or disqualify himself from governmental decisions which may materially affect such assets. However, if the trust were a source of income to the trustee by virtue of payments made to him for administering the trust, he would be required to disclose the trust as a source of income and disqualify himself from any governmental decision which may materially affect the trust. He still would not have to dis- close the assets of the trust nor disqualify himself from govern- mental decisions which could materially affect an individual trust asset but which would not materially affect the trust as a whole.
embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that a public official serving as a trustee with legal title to trust assets need not disclose or disqualify from decisions affecting those assets, unless the trust provides income to the official.
